title: Needle-felted butterfly
subtitle: A flat decorative butterfly approximately 10 cm across, in two-tone merino with wire-stiffened wings
excerpt: Needle-felt four flat wing shapes in merino roving, add surface markings with contrast colours, and mount them on a short wire body. The finished butterfly can be pinned as a brooch, wired into a plant, or hung as a decoration. Each butterfly uses around 10 g of roving.
sourceNotes: Flat needle-felted butterfly forms with wire bodies are a standard beginner sculpture project in European felting guilds. Observation-led; the form is consistent across introductory teaching materials.

glossaryTerms.definition: A dry felting method using barbed needles that tangle wool fibres into a dense flat or three-dimensional form without water or heat.
Combed and drawn wool fibres in a continuous rope, ready for felting.

content.text: This butterfly is built as four flat felt wings joined by a wire body. The wings are

flat on a foam mat, trimmed to wing shape with scissors, and then marked with surface spots and veining in contrast colours. A length of florist wire is folded to form the body and bent to the correct angle; the wing pairs are placed over the wire and needle-bonded to it. The finished butterfly lies flat and can be pinned or wired into almost any surface. Use
in two strongly contrasting colours for the most visible wing marking.

attrs.title: Needle safety
attrs.body: Stab straight in and out at 90 degrees. Never lever sideways. Keep fingers clear of the needle path.

items.name: Merino roving, wing colour (such as orange or purple), 8 g
Merino roving, contrast colour for markings (such as black or white), 2 g
Florist wire or craft wire, 0.8 mm gauge, 20 cm

content.text: Pull a wisp of wing-colour roving and lay it flat on the foam mat in a rough oval approximately 8 x 5 cm. Needle across the surface with the 38-gauge needle until it is a flat, even panel. Build up a second thin layer on top and needle it in. Repeat for all four wings (two larger upper wings and two smaller lower wings): upper wings approximately 8 x 5 cm, lower wings approximately 5 x 3 cm. Each panel should feel flat and firm, with no springy areas.

Draw a wing outline on each flat panel with a felt-tip pen and cut it out with scissors. Upper wings are a rounded teardrop shape, widest at the top and narrowing to a point toward the body. Lower wings are a shorter, broader teardrop. Scissors cut cleanly through well-felted panels; if the edge frays during cutting, the panel needs more needling before trimming.

Pull tiny pinches of contrast-colour roving and needle them onto the wing surfaces using the fine 40-gauge needle. Common butterfly markings are: small circular spots near the wing tips, a thin border line around the edge of each wing, and a veining of fine lines radiating from the body edge. Place each wisp and needle it down precisely; the fine needle gives more control than the coarser one.

Fold the wire in half and twist the two halves together from the fold downward for 3 cm to form the body. Bend the free ends outward at 45 degrees to form the antennae. Lay the upper wing pair with the straight inner edges touching the wire body, then the lower wing pair below them. Needle through the inner edge of each wing into the wire and through to the foam mat to bond the felt to the wire. Cover the wire body and wing joins with a thin layer of dark roving needled in place. Attach a brooch back to the wire if mounting as a pin.

items.problem: The wing edges fray when cut with scissors
items.cause: The panel was not felted densely enough before trimming
items.fix: Needle the panel more thoroughly before cutting. Well-felted merino cuts cleanly with scissors and does not fray.

items.problem: The surface markings spread beyond the intended shape
items.cause: The contrast wisps were too thick or the needling spread them outward
items.fix: Use the finest possible wisps, rolled into a compact shape before placing. Needle from directly above with the fine needle and keep stabs within the mark boundary.
